pain_points: IPS Related
Historical Record Management: Historical permits and property records often require searching IPS together with OnBase because historical information is not consolidated.
Incomplete Property History: IPS does not always provide a complete historical picture for permits, Certificates of Occupancy, or property changes.
Large Plan Viewing: Large plans and surveys cannot be viewed effectively within IPS.
Limited Historical Visibility: Historical records are frequently needed to determine legal occupancy and previous property conditions but are not always easily accessible.
Other
Property Research Complexity: Assessment work frequently requires combining information from permits, surveys, Engineering, and historical records to understand complete property history.
Subdivision Tracking: Following parcel history after resubdivisions requires coordination with Engineering and County mapping records.
Permit Dependency: Assessment activities depend heavily on permit activity to identify changes affecting assessed value, although unpermitted improvements can still occur.
Seasonal Workload: Assessment activities vary throughout the year based on tax deadlines, permit volume, and market activity.
IPS Related
System Fragmentation
Split Permit & Code Systems: Permit and Code Enforcement information exists in separate systems and workflows.
Scattered Information: Property and permit information is distributed across IPS, Camino, AS400, OnBase, and historical archives.
Research Complexity: Users must search multiple systems to understand complete property history.
Difficult Information Retrieval: Finding relevant information often feels like "hunting" through multiple systems.
Integration Limitations
Limited IPS-Camino Integration: Information does not flow seamlessly between systems.
No Shared Permit/Violation Visibility: Permit reviewers cannot easily see related code information.
Duplicate Research Effort: Staff repeatedly search for the same information across systems.
No Automatic Cross-System Notifications: Permit and code workflows do not automatically notify each other.
Applicant Visibility Issues
Limited Status Transparency: Applicants cannot easily see permit status and review progress.
Review Status Gaps: Difficult for applicants to understand where applications are within the review process.
Communication Burden: Staff spend significant time responding to status inquiries.
Other
Application & Review Challenges
Too Many Applications: Large permit volumes create administrative workload.
Incorrect Document Uploads: Applicants frequently upload incorrect or incomplete documentation.
Review Delays: Applications can stall while waiting on reviews from other departments.
Manual Tracking Requirements: Staff use spreadsheets and external tools to monitor progress.
Documentation Challenges
Documentation Gaps: Existing systems do not adequately support permit documentation needs.
Historical Records Across Multiple Sources: Research requires navigating legacy repositories.
No Centralized Repository: Information is spread across numerous databases and archives.
Coordination Challenges
Cross-Department Dependencies: Progress depends on multiple departments completing reviews.
Permit-to-Code Disconnect: Code Enforcement and permitting activities are not fully synchronized.
Reviewer Coordination: Managing comments and approvals from multiple reviewers is time-consuming.
IPS related:
System Stability
System Crashes: IPS crashes during high-volume processing activities.
Attachment Issues: Problems saving and managing attachments.
Slow Performance: Delays when processing applications and notices.
Workflow Interruptions: Certain activities require workarounds or manual intervention.
Case Management Limitations
Complex Navigation: Multiple screens and tabs create inefficiencies.
Duplicate Ownership Records: Ownership information is difficult to maintain.
Expiration Tracking Limitations: Renewal tracking depends heavily on reports.
Address Lookup Challenges: Difficulty locating accurate address information.
Information Management Challenges
Manual Ownership Maintenance: Significant effort required to maintain ownership records.
No Automated Renewal Queue: Renewal management requires manual tracking.
Property Research Complexity: Multiple searches are often required to locate information.
Other
Camino Challenges
Duplicate Data Entry: Information must often be entered into both IPS and Camino.
Poor Integration: Limited integration between Camino and IPS.
Split Workflows: Licensing, permitting, and registry processes span multiple systems.
Application Processing Complexity: Certain workflows became more complicated after Camino implementation.
Scheduling & Communication Challenges
Manual Scheduling: Significant effort required to coordinate inspections.
Applicant Status Requests: Staff spend considerable time responding to status inquiries.
Manual Notification Processes: Renewal and expiration notices require manual effort.
Applicant Confusion: Applicants often struggle to understand requirements and application status.
Operational Challenges
Spreadsheet Dependency: External spreadsheets are required to track work.
Cross-System Research: Information must be gathered from several systems.
Duplicate Processes: Similar information is maintained in multiple locations.

System Stability
IPS crashes frequently during complaint entry and daily operations.
Users experience unhandled exceptions and memory-related errors.
Work can be lost when the system crashes unexpectedly.

Inspection appointments must be entered into multiple systems.
Outlook and IPS schedules are not synchronized.
Periodic Inspection scheduling workflows are cumbersome.
Inspector lists in the Periodic Inspection module are not alphabetized.
Case Management Challenges
Finding specific cases can be difficult.
Parcel History provides limited information when reviewing large properties.
Important information can be difficult to locate within cases.
Case notes and attachments are not always organized consistently.
Workflow Challenges

Save functions are inconsistent.
Certain attachments cannot always be uploaded successfully.
Some complaint types do not have clearly defined workflows.
Performance Issues
Complaint cases can take significant time to open.
System responsiveness slows when handling multiple activities simultaneously.
Uploading documents can impact performance.
Other
Phone System Issues
Calls automatically disconnect after several minutes.
Long scheduling conversations are frequently interrupted.
Customers become frustrated when calls are dropped.
Staff lose significant time handling repeat calls.
Multiple Systems

Duplicate data entry is required across multiple applications.
Staff must frequently switch between systems to complete a task.

Address changes are not always reflected consistently.

Workload Challenges

Front-counter traffic can become overwhelming.

Knowledge of certain processes is dependent on individual staff experience.
Limited standardization exists across some administrative workflows.

BAA Process Challenges
Tickets are frequently returned for minor corrections.
Multiple review cycles delay enforcement activities.

IPS Related
System Stability
Frequent Crashes: IPS crashes regularly, sometimes multiple times per week.
Session Loss: Work can be lost when IPS crashes unexpectedly.
Performance Issues: System becomes slower as case volume increases.
Ownership & Property Information
Ownership Issues: Ownership records are inconsistent and difficult to maintain.
Multiple Record Locations: Ownership information can exist in several places.
Outdated Information: Contact and ownership information is not always current.
Address Discrepancies: Property addresses are sometimes inconsistent across systems.
Case Management Limitations
Cases Difficult to Find: Locating specific cases can be time-consuming.
Too Much Information in Different Areas: Important information is spread throughout the system.
Multiple Ways to Update Data: Different update paths create inconsistencies.
Case Visibility Issues: Limited visibility into activities completed by other departments.
Tickle Dates Can Be Missed: Reminder dates can be overlooked or lost.
Missing Information
No Elevator Certification Visibility: Elevator certification status is difficult to access.
No Water Certification Visibility: Water certification information is not easily available.
No Sprinkler Report Access: Inspectors cannot easily access sprinkler system reports.
Zoning Information Gaps: Zoning reviews do not consistently appear within inspection cases.
Third-Party Information Missing: Important compliance documentation exists outside IPS.
Other
Multiple Systems
Too Many Applications: Inspectors must switch between IPS, Camino, AS400, Compliance Engine, Outlook, and other tools.
No Single Source of Truth: Information needed for inspections is spread across multiple systems.
Repeated Research: The same information often must be searched in several locations.
Communication & Coordination
Slow Zoning Approvals: Zone checks often delay certificate issuance.
Lack of Updates from Other Departments: Fire, Zoning, Law, and BAA do not consistently update case information.
Reliance on Others: Inspectors depend heavily on other departments to complete work.
BAA Process Delays: Inspectors are often blamed for delays caused by BAA or Law.
Permit Communication Gaps: Permit requirements are not always communicated effectively.
Field Technology Limitations
Tablet Reliability Issues: Existing tablets are slow and unreliable.
Mobile Access Limitations: IPS functionality in the field is limited.
Need for Personal Hotspots: Inspectors sometimes rely on personal hotspots to connect.
Field Documentation Challenges: Lack of effective mobile tools increases office-based data entry.
IPS Related
System Performance
System Buffering & Slowdowns: IPS experiences performance issues and occasional breakdowns.
Searching Difficulties: Finding information can be time-consuming.
Limited Documentation Capabilities: Difficult to document certain inspection and permit activities.
Field Access Limitations: Limited ability to access and update cases in the field.
Manual Scheduling Requirements: Inspectors often maintain schedules outside IPS.
Case Management Limitations
No Permit Workflow Integration: Permit workflows are primarily managed outside IPS.
Lack of Permit Tracking Functionality: Permit inspectors rely heavily on Camino instead of IPS.
Limited Follow-Up Tracking: IPS does not adequately support permit inspection workflows.
Difficulty Managing Inspection Histories: Inspectors must manually review large volumes of information.
Data & Information Challenges
Searching for Information: Important project and permit information can be difficult to locate.
No Unified Permit Record: Information is spread across multiple systems.
Project Visibility Issues: Inspectors lack visibility into all project activities from one location.
Other
Camino Challenges
Scheduling Disconnect: Inspections scheduled in Camino do not automatically appear in Outlook.
No Calendar Integration: Inspectors maintain duplicate schedules.
No Tickler Functionality: Camino lacks reminder and follow-up tracking capabilities.
Address Management Issues: Address information can be inaccurate or difficult to manage.
Duplicate Records: Duplicate entries exist within Camino.
Navigation Difficulties: Users find Camino difficult to navigate.
Limited Visibility: Difficult to see assigned work and active projects.
No Internal Reminder System: Inspectors rely on manual notes and tracking methods.
Internet Dependency: Some functions are unavailable when connectivity issues occur.
Permit Office Coordination Issues: Permit processes are not fully integrated.
Communication Challenges
Contractor Coordination: Contractors do not always respond promptly to communication.
Lack of Coordination: Electrical inspectors identified limited coordination across teams.
Training Gaps: Insufficient training on Camino and related processes.
Manual Follow-Up Requirements: Inspectors must manually track contractor responses and inspection needs.
Operational Challenges
Handwritten Tracking: Inspectors often maintain handwritten schedules and inspection notes.
Multiple Systems: Staff must switch between Camino, IPS, Outlook, phones, and other tools.
Duplicate Data Entry: Information must be entered and tracked in multiple locations.
Limited Mobile Functionality: Field-based work remains partially paper-driven.
IPS related
Property Research Difficulties
Historical Research Takes Too Long: Researching prior violations, occupancy records, and property history requires multiple searches and system access.
Difficult Certificate Research: Finding previous Certificates of Occupancy and capacity information requires extensive digging.
Multiple Search Locations: Information exists in several areas of the system.
Parcel Search Limitations: Parcel information must be exact and is difficult to locate.
Information Fragmentation
Multiple Documentation Locations: Users document actions, inspections, photos, and violations in different places.
Inconsistent Documentation Practices: Different users store information differently.
Missing Parcel Information: Important parcel data is unavailable or difficult to locate.
Inactive Parcel Issues: Parcels that are active appear inactive within IPS.
Certificate & Inspection Review Challenges
CofO Sign-Off Process: Reviewing Certificates of Occupancy requires checking multiple locations.
Closed Inspection Visibility: Difficult to locate all completed inspections associated with a project.
Review Tracking Challenges: Tracking inspection and permit review status is cumbersome.
Remote Access Limitations
Desktop Dependency: IPS does not function well outside the office.
No Effective Mobile Access: Inspectors cannot effectively use IPS in the field.
Limited Real-Time Documentation: Inspection information is not easily entered from mobile devices.
Other
Scheduling Challenges
No Built-In Calendar: Camino and EPR lack effective scheduling and calendar functionality.
Multiple Scheduling Systems: Staff use Outlook, ERP, and permit systems (Camino) simultaneously.
Duplicate Scheduling Activities: Inspection scheduling must be maintained in multiple locations.
System Integration Challenges
Too Many Systems: Staff use IPS, Camino, EPR, Compliance Engine, Outlook, spreadsheets, and paper planners.
Weak Integration: Information does not move efficiently between systems.
Manual Coordination: Significant effort is required to coordinate data across platforms.
Operational Challenges
Large Volume of Documentation: Managing plans, inspections, corrections, and approvals is time-consuming.
Permit Review Tracking: Monitoring project reviews requires manual follow-up.
Property Identification Issues: Properties with multiple businesses or occupancies are difficult to manage.
Address Matching Challenges: Searches require exact addresses.
IPS Related:
System Performance
System Freezing: IPS frequently freezes or experiences loading delays.
Slow Performance: Opening cases and completing actions can be slow.
System Shutdowns: IPS occasionally requires restarts or intervention to function properly.
Remote Access Issues: Difficulties connecting to IPS remotely from the field.
Case Management Limitations
No Global Case View: Difficult to review all active and closed cases associated with a property.
Case Navigation Complexity: Navigating cases requires extensive system knowledge and experience.
Too Many Actions: Large numbers of actions and dropdown options create inefficiencies.
No Case Categorization: Cases cannot easily be categorized by status such as drive-by inspections, waiting on owners, or follow-up activities.
No Automated Case Monitoring: Difficult to identify cases lacking inspection dates or pending reviews.
Data & Information Challenges
Ownership Information Issues: Ownership information is not always updated consistently.
Address Management Problems: Address changes do not automatically update across all cases.
No Unified Address Book: Property and ownership information can vary between systems.
Lead Map Accessibility: Lead-risk information is not readily available within cases.
Limited Water Information Access: Water records are not available directly in IPS.
Information Fragmentation: Important information exists in multiple systems and locations.
Field Functionality Limitations
Limited Field Access: Inspectors cannot fully access or update IPS while in the field.
Photo Upload Process: Uploading and organizing photos is cumbersome.
No Mobile Workflow: Inspectors must often return to the office to complete documentation.
No Laptop Access: Limited ability to update cases during inspections.
Other:
Multiple Systems
Too Many Systems: Inspectors must use IPS, AS400, GIS, Outlook, Dropbox, spreadsheets, lead maps, and external databases.
Repeated Research: Ownership, water, and lead information require separate searches.
No Single Source of Truth: Property information is spread across multiple applications.
Communication Challenges
Coordination Delays: Delays occur when waiting for information from other staff or departments.
Shared Data Issues: Information entered by multiple users can create delays or confusion.
Address Updates: Address corrections are not consistently communicated throughout systems.
Operational Challenges
Manual Tracking: Inspectors maintain spreadsheets and notes to manage workloads.
Lead Map Verification: Frequent manual checking of lead maps.
Violation Entry Delays: Simultaneous data entry can slow violation processing.
Large Dropdown Lists: Long dropdown menus make data entry inefficient.
Case Organization Difficulties: Difficult to distinguish between different case types and statuses.

IPS Related:
Case Management Gaps
Missed Case Tracking: Cases can be missed if tickle dates are not entered correctly.
No Automatic Monitoring: No system-generated alerts for inactive or stalled cases.
Open Case Cleanup: Large number of legacy and inactive cases require manual cleanup.
Cases Easily Lost: Cases, permits, and assignments can disappear from visibility if workflows are not followed correctly.
Human Error Dependency: The system relies heavily on users remembering to complete actions.
Data & Information Issues
Incomplete Historical Data: Historical information is missing or difficult to locate.
Difficult Property Research: Researching property history requires navigating multiple records and screens.
Parcel Search Challenges: Subdivided and inactive parcels are difficult to track.
Scattered Information: Related property information is not presented in a single view.
Reporting Limitations
Limited Reporting Capability: Reporting and analysis require significant manual effort.
No Advanced Analytics: Limited ability to identify trends, recurring offenders, or enforcement patterns.
System Performance & Usability
Volume Challenges: IPS becomes cumbersome as case volume increases.
Specialized Knowledge Required: Users require significant experience to fully utilize the system.
No Batch Processing: Large-scale updates and cleanup activities are difficult to perform.
Other
Multiple Systems
Too Many Systems: Staff must use IPS, Camino, AS400, OnBase, GIS, Compliance Engine, spreadsheets, and other tools to complete a single workflow.
Duplicate Data Entry: Information must be entered and verified across multiple applications.
No Single Source of Truth: Property information is distributed across multiple databases.
Historical Information
Missing Historical Records: Some historical records were lost or remain inaccessible.
Paper-Based Archives: Information remains stored in microfiche, paper files, and legacy systems.
Incomplete Digital Migration: Historical information has not been fully digitized.
Communication Challenges
Email Overload: Excessive reliance on email for project management and collaboration.
Cross-Department Visibility: Difficult to understand case status once another department becomes involved.
Coordination Delays: Multiple departments are involved in many workflows, creating delays.
Contractor & Public Challenges
Camino Frustration: Contractors frequently struggle with Camino.
Address Discrepancies: Property addresses often differ between systems, creating confusion.
Constituent Escalations: Supervisors spend significant time responding to escalated complaints and requests.
IPS related:
Fragmented Historical Records: Historical property, zoning, and parcel records are scattered across IPS, Camino, OnBase, AS400, microfiche, and paper archives, making research time-consuming.
Complex Property Research: Parcel history is difficult to follow after subdivisions, lot alterations, ownership changes, and address changes.
Incomplete Digital Records: Many historical zoning projects and approvals have not been migrated into IPS, requiring staff to search legacy systems.
Limited Search Functionality: Searching historical parcels, addresses, and zoning records is inefficient due to limited filtering and search capabilities.
Scattered Project Information: Project documents, approvals, and historical records are distributed across multiple systems, making it difficult to view complete project history.
Missing Supporting Documentation: Older zoning projects often contain incomplete attachments or missing supporting documents within IPS.
Other:
Late Zoning Reviews: Certificate of Use and zoning reviews sometimes occur too late in the permitting process, delaying approvals.
Manual Research Process: Staff must switch between multiple systems and physical archives to complete property research.
High Communication Volume: Responding to applicant inquiries and coordinating with reviewing departments consumes significant staff time.
Cross-Department Dependencies: Zoning reviews depend on timely input and coordination from multiple City departments.
Legacy Record Dependency: Historical paper files and basement archives are still required to complete many zoning reviews.
IPS related:
Incomplete Case Documentation: Legal work cannot proceed when notices, Nail & Mail documentation, photographs, or inspection records have not been uploaded into IPS.
Underutilized System: IPS is valuable when fully utilized, but inconsistent use by Code Enforcement results in incomplete notes and missing documentation.
Limited Property Overview: IPS cannot easily combine multiple complaints and violations into a comprehensive property-level view.
External Data Dependency: Water records require AS400 and tax information requires external systems because they are not integrated into IPS.
Limited Document Export: IPS information cannot easily transfer into Microsoft Word for preparing legal documents.
Difficult Record Review: The Action Notes layout makes reviewing case history difficult because information is not presented clearly.
Other:
Documentation Quality: Court cases are delayed when inspectors do not consistently upload documents, photographs, or complete notes.
Manual Legal Research: Ownership, tax, and legal research require multiple external systems including Westlaw, County Clerk, eTax, and AS400.
Cross-Department Dependencies: Legal work depends on inspectors completing investigations and providing complete documentation before cases can proceed.
Evidence Collection: Gathering exhibits and supporting documentation requires manual coordination across multiple departments and file repositories.
